title: Percent, Rates, Markups, Discounts, and Repeated Change · Assumption Clinic
summary: Audit prices, discounts, taxes, tips, population changes, and performance claims. Learners isolate plausible failures, compare competing hypotheses, and record cause-level repair evidence. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in a consumer percent-claim audit and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Identify the comparison base before calculating a percent, percentage, or whole.
Calculate absolute and relative change and explain when each comparison is useful.
Model markup, discount, tax, and tip as ordered multiplicative factors.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.python:
"""applied-mathematics-beginner cumulative model portfolio."""

from math import exp, log, sqrt


def reasonableness(value: float, lower: float, upper: float) -> bool:
    return lower <= value <= upper

# Add one tested model increment per activity.

steps.title: Order the regression proof
steps.instruction: Order the Percent Claims evidence cycle before implementing it.
steps.why: Correct order keeps Percent Claims assumptions visible before execution and verifies its result against a boundary case afterward.

content.text: Detect denominator switching, percentage-point confusion, and selective baselines in public claims. Use this as an observable contract: name its preconditions, trace the state or value change, and state what evidence would disprove the result.

content.title: Reasoning move
content.text: Compare plausible approaches against the decision, constraints, evidence quality, and cost of being wrong.

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: Use a changed worked example case derived from this module: math beginner percent change. Preserve inputs, constraints, an observable trace, and a boundary result. A defensible solution applies Percent Claims, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to a consumer percent-claim audit.

content.title: Non-worked case
content.text: Percent and percentage-point differences are interchangeable. This fails the Percent Claims contract because it omits a precondition, invariant, ownership rule, or counterexample needed by this exact case.

steps.title: Recalculate the repaired case
steps.instruction: A price of $90 is reduced by 12%. What is the new price?
steps.why: A numerical result is not complete until its unit, assumptions, scale, and tolerance survive a reasonableness check.

content.text: Use 90 × (1 − 12/100), then compare the result with the original price.

content.title: Before calculating
content.text: Predict the sign and approximate magnitude. Enter the requested quantity with a unit, then compare it with that prediction.

hints: Use 90 × (1 − 12/100), then compare the result with the original price.
Keep full working precision and round only the final reported value.
The accepted result is within 0.01 of the exact value and includes $ or dollars.

steps.title: Publish the incident record
steps.instruction: Write a concise evidence note that explains when Percent Claims applies, what can fail, and how the changed case was verified.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Detect denominator switching, percentage-point confusion, and selective baselines in public claims.

checks.failureMessage: Frame the decision before modeling, and validate after obtaining a result.

checks.description: The evidence note explains the model, failure risk, and verification in the learner’s own words.
